questions.questionText: The neurotransmitter associated with Parkinson's disease (deficiency in nigrostriatal pathway) is:
questions.explanation: Parkinson's disease: degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra pars compacta → decreased dopamine in the nigrostriatal pathway → movement disorder (bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, postural instability). Treatment: L-DOPA (dopamine precursor crosses BBB), dopamine agonists. Loss of DA in mesolimbic pathway → depression. Excess DA in mesolimbic → schizophrenia (positive symptoms). Reference: Guyton — Basal Ganglia.

questions.questionText: The primary sensory cortex is located in the:
questions.explanation: Somatosensory cortex: postcentral gyrus (parietal lobe, Brodmann areas 3,1,2). Primary motor cortex: precentral gyrus (frontal lobe, area 4). Broca's area: inferior frontal gyrus (area 44,45 — motor speech). Wernicke's area: superior temporal gyrus (area 22 — comprehension). Primary visual cortex: occipital lobe (area 17). Auditory cortex: superior temporal gyrus (areas 41,42). Somatosensory has homunculus representation. Reference: Guyton — Cortical Areas.

questions.questionText: The sleep stage associated with vivid dreams and rapid eye movements is:
questions.explanation: REM (rapid eye movement) sleep: vivid dreams, desynchronized EEG (similar to awake state — paradoxical sleep), rapid eye movements, skeletal muscle atonia (except diaphragm and eye muscles), increased brain O2 consumption. NREM stages: Stage 1 (light, theta waves), Stage 2 (sleep spindles, K-complexes), Stage 3 (deep/slow-wave sleep, delta waves — restorative). Sleep cycles: 90-minute cycles, REM increases toward morning. Reference: Guyton — Sleep Stages.

questions.questionText: The EEG pattern during deep sleep shows:
questions.explanation: EEG waves: Beta (>14 Hz): awake, alert, active thinking. Alpha (8-13 Hz): relaxed, awake, eyes closed. Theta (4-7 Hz): light sleep (stage 1), drowsiness, also normal in children. Delta (<4 Hz, high amplitude): deep sleep (stage 3/4). Beta desynchronized pattern also appears in REM sleep. Alpha waves disappear when eyes open (alpha block). Epileptic seizures: spike-and-wave pattern on EEG. Reference: Guyton — EEG Rhythms.

questions.questionText: Broca's aphasia (motor/expressive aphasia) results from damage to:
questions.optionB: Broca's area in inferior frontal gyrus (areas 44,45)
questions.explanation: Broca's area (dominant hemisphere, usually left): motor speech production. Damage → non-fluent aphasia: speech is effortful, telegraphic, poor articulation, but comprehension is intact. Wernicke's area (posterior superior temporal): comprehension. Damage → fluent aphasia: speech fluent but meaningless ('word salad'), poor comprehension. Conduction aphasia: arcuate fasciculus damage → repetition deficit, comprehension and fluency intact. Global aphasia: both areas damaged. Reference: Guyton — Aphasia.

questions.questionText: The reticular activating system (RAS) is responsible for:
questions.explanation: RAS (reticular formation in brainstem): maintains wakefulness and arousal by diffuse projections to thalamus and cortex. Damage → coma (irreversible unconsciousness). Ascending RAS: wakefulness (uses ACh, norepinephrine, serotonin, dopamine, histamine). Descending reticular formation: modulates pain, muscle tone, autonomic functions. General anesthetics depress RAS. Persistent vegetative state: cortex damaged but RAS intact (awake but unaware). Reference: Guyton — RAS.

questions.questionText: The cerebellum is primarily involved in:
questions.optionB: Coordination, balance, motor learning, and error correction
questions.explanation: Cerebellum functions: coordinate smooth, precise movements, maintain posture and balance (vestibulocerebellum), plan and time movements (cerebrocerebellum), motor learning (error correction). Damage: ataxia (NOT paralysis), dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, cerebellar nystagmus, scanning/staccato speech, hypotonia, pendular reflexes. Lesions affect IPSILATERAL side (unlike cerebral lesions which affect contralateral). Reference: Guyton — Cerebellum.

questions.questionText: Pain sensation is carried by:
questions.optionB: Aδ fibers (fast/sharp pain) and C fibers (slow/dull pain)
questions.explanation: Pain pathways: Aδ fibers (thinly myelinated, 5-30 m/s): fast, sharp, well-localized (first pain). C fibers (unmyelinated, 0.5-2 m/s): slow, dull, burning, poorly localized (second pain). Enter spinal cord → synapse in dorsal horn (substantia gelatinosa) → cross midline → ascend in lateral spinothalamic tract → thalamus (VPL nucleus) → somatosensory cortex. Gate control theory: large fiber (Aβ) stimulation can 'close the gate' to pain. Reference: Guyton — Pain Pathways.

questions.questionText: The hypothalamus controls all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Hypothalamus: master controller of autonomic functions, endocrine regulation, homeostasis. Functions: temperature (anterior = cooling, posterior = heating), hunger (lateral = feeding center, ventromedial = satiety center), thirst (osmoreceptors), circadian rhythm (suprachiasmatic nucleus), emotion/behavior, ADH/oxytocin production, releasing/inhibiting hormones for pituitary. Voluntary skilled movements are controlled by motor cortex, basal ganglia, cerebellum. Reference: Guyton — Hypothalamus.

questions.questionText: Referred pain is pain perceived in a location distant from the actual site of pathology, due to:
questions.optionA: Direct nerve damage at the referred site
questions.optionB: Convergence of visceral and somatic afferents on the same spinal cord neurons
questions.explanation: Referred pain: visceral afferents converge with somatic afferents on shared second-order neurons in the dorsal horn → brain misinterprets the origin as somatic. Examples: heart → left arm/jaw (T1-T5), diaphragm → shoulder (C3-C5 phrenic), gallbladder → right shoulder, appendix → periumbilical (early) → RIF (later), kidney → groin/flank. Important for diagnosis of visceral pathology. Reference: Guyton — Referred Pain.

questions.questionText: The neurotransmitter at the preganglionic sympathetic AND parasympathetic neurons is:
questions.explanation: ACh is the neurotransmitter at ALL preganglionic neurons (both sympathetic and parasympathetic) and all parasympathetic postganglionic neurons. Norepinephrine is the neurotransmitter at most sympathetic postganglionic neurons (exception: sympathetic to sweat glands and adrenal medulla use ACh). Adrenal medulla: releases epinephrine (80%) and norepinephrine (20%) directly into blood. Reference: Guyton — ANS Neurotransmitters.

questions.questionText: Long-term potentiation (LTP) in the hippocampus involves which receptor?
questions.explanation: LTP (cellular basis of memory and learning): repeated stimulation → AMPA receptor activation → depolarization → Mg2+ block removed from NMDA receptor → NMDA opens → Ca2+ influx → activates CaMKII, PKC → synaptic strengthening. NMDA receptor is a coincidence detector (requires both presynaptic glutamate AND postsynaptic depolarization). Hippocampus is critical for declarative (explicit) memory formation. Damage: anterograde amnesia. Reference: Guyton — LTP Memory.

questions.questionText: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) is formed primarily by:
questions.optionB: Tight junctions between cerebral capillary endothelial cells
questions.explanation: BBB: tight junctions (zona occludens) between cerebral capillary endothelial cells — most important structural component. Astrocyte foot processes contribute to BBB induction and maintenance. BBB is selective: freely permeable to lipid-soluble substances (O2, CO2, ethanol, anesthetic gases) and glucose (via GLUT1). Impermeable to most drugs, large molecules, charged particles. Absent in circumventricular organs. Breaks down in inflammation, tumors, hypertensive crises. Reference: Guyton — BBB.

questions.questionText: The lateral spinothalamic tract carries:
questions.explanation: Lateral spinothalamic tract: pain and temperature. Crosses at the spinal cord level (within 1-2 segments). Anterior spinothalamic: crude touch and pressure. Dorsal (posterior) columns (fasciculus gracilis and cuneatus): fine touch, vibration, proprioception, 2-point discrimination — cross at medulla (medial lemniscus). Syringomyelia (central cord cavity): damages crossing fibers of lateral spinothalamic → bilateral loss of pain/temperature ('cape-like'), preserved touch. Reference: Guyton — Spinal Tracts.

questions.questionText: CSF is mainly produced by:
questions.explanation: CSF: produced by choroid plexus (~500 mL/day, volume at any time ~150 mL, replaced 3-4 times/day). Flows: lateral ventricles → interventricular foramen (Monro) → third ventricle → cerebral aqueduct (Sylvius) → fourth ventricle → foramen of Magendie/Luschka → subarachnoid space → absorbed by arachnoid granulations (villi) into dural venous sinuses. Normal pressure: 70-180 mmH2O. Obstruction → hydrocephalus. Reference: Guyton — CSF.

questions.questionText: The reward/pleasure pathway in the brain is primarily mediated by:
questions.optionB: Mesolimbic dopamine pathway (VTA to nucleus accumbens)
questions.explanation: Mesolimbic pathway: dopaminergic neurons from VTA (ventral tegmental area) to nucleus accumbens (ventral striatum) → mediates reward, pleasure, reinforcement, motivation. Drugs of abuse (cocaine, amphetamines, opioids, nicotine, alcohol) all increase dopamine in this pathway. Mesocortical pathway (VTA → prefrontal cortex): executive function, motivation. Dysfunction → negative symptoms of schizophrenia, depression. Reference: Guyton — Dopamine Reward.

questions.questionText: The corticospinal (pyramidal) tract crosses at the:
questions.optionB: Pyramidal decussation in the lower medulla
questions.explanation: Corticospinal tract (main motor pathway): ~85% of fibers cross at the pyramidal decussation in the caudal medulla → lateral corticospinal tract (controls distal muscles/fine movements). ~15% remain uncrossed → anterior corticospinal tract (crosses at the spinal level, controls proximal/axial muscles). This explains why cortical lesions cause CONTRALATERAL motor deficits. Upper motor neuron signs: spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski positive, clonus. Reference: Guyton — Corticospinal Tract.

questions.questionText: The endogenous opioids (endorphins, enkephalins) modulate pain by:
questions.optionB: Inhibiting pain transmission at the dorsal horn (gate control mechanism)
questions.explanation: Endogenous opioids (β-endorphin from POMC, enkephalins, dynorphins): bind opioid receptors (μ, δ, κ) in the PAG (periaqueductal gray), raphe nuclei, dorsal horn. Mechanism: inhibit substance P release from primary afferents, hyperpolarize second-order neurons → block pain transmission. Descending pain modulation: PAG → raphe nuclei (serotonin) and locus coeruleus (norepinephrine) → dorsal horn → pain inhibition. Morphine mimics this system. Reference: Guyton — Endogenous Analgesia.

questions.questionText: The sensory homunculus on the postcentral gyrus shows disproportionately large representation for:
questions.explanation: The sensory homunculus (and motor homunculus) shows that cortical representation is proportional to the density of sensory innervation and functional importance, NOT body size. Hands, lips, face, and tongue have the LARGEST cortical representation due to highest receptor density and finest sensory discrimination. Trunk has relatively small representation. Similarly in motor cortex, hand and face have largest areas (fine motor control). Reference: Guyton — Somatosensory Cortex.

questions.questionText: Upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Spastic paralysis, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign positive, clonus
questions.explanation: UMN lesion signs (above anterior horn cell): spastic paralysis (velocity-dependent increase in tone), hyperreflexia, Babinski sign (extensor plantar response), clonus, no significant muscle atrophy (disuse atrophy may occur late), no fasciculations. LMN lesion signs (anterior horn cell and below): flaccid paralysis, hyporeflexia/areflexia, muscle atrophy, fasciculations. Clasp-knife rigidity in UMN vs lead-pipe/cogwheel in extrapyramidal. Reference: Guyton — UMN vs LMN.

questions.questionText: The vestibular system detects:
questions.explanation: Vestibular system (in inner ear): (1) Semicircular canals (3 pairs, perpendicular planes): detect ANGULAR acceleration (head rotation) via endolymph flow moving the cupula/hair cells. (2) Otolith organs — utricle (horizontal linear acceleration/head tilt) and saccule (vertical linear acceleration, gravity) via otoconia on the macular hair cells. Vestibular nerve (CN VIII) → vestibular nuclei → cerebellum, oculomotor, spinal cord. Damage → vertigo, nystagmus, imbalance. Reference: Guyton — Vestibular.

questions.questionText: Glutamate is the primary excitatory neurotransmitter in the CNS, while the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter is:
questions.explanation: Glutamate: main excitatory NT in CNS (acts on NMDA, AMPA, kainate receptors). GABA: main inhibitory NT in the BRAIN (acts on GABA-A: Cl- channel → hyperpolarization; GABA-B: K+ channel, GPCR). Glycine: main inhibitory NT in the spinal cord. Benzodiazepines and barbiturates enhance GABA-A function. Excitotoxicity (excess glutamate): neuronal death in stroke, trauma, neurodegenerative diseases. Reference: Guyton — Neurotransmitters.

questions.questionText: The corneal reflex tests the integrity of which cranial nerves?
questions.optionB: CN V (afferent, ophthalmic division) and CN VII (efferent, orbicularis oculi)
questions.explanation: Corneal reflex: touch cornea → afferent signal via V1 (ophthalmic division of trigeminal nerve) → efferent via CN VII (facial nerve) → blink (orbicularis oculi contraction). Both direct (same side) and consensual (opposite side) blink occurs. Absent corneal reflex indicates CN V or CN VII lesion, or deep coma (brainstem death assessment). CN IX/X (afferent/efferent) mediate the gag reflex. Reference: Guyton — Cranial Nerve Reflexes.

questions.questionText: Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection of spinal cord) presents with:
questions.optionB: Ipsilateral motor loss + proprioception loss; contralateral pain/temperature loss
questions.explanation: Brown-Séquard (cord hemisection): Ipsilateral: UMN paralysis below lesion (corticospinal tract — crosses at medulla), loss of proprioception/vibration/fine touch (dorsal columns — cross at medulla). Contralateral: loss of pain/temperature 1-2 levels below (lateral spinothalamic tract — crosses at spinal level). Ipsilateral LMN signs at level of lesion (anterior horn damage). Best prognosis of incomplete cord syndromes. Reference: Guyton — Brown-Séquard.

questions.questionText: The visual cortex is located in the:
questions.optionC: Occipital lobe (around calcarine sulcus, Brodmann area 17)
questions.explanation: Primary visual cortex: Brodmann area 17, along the calcarine sulcus in the occipital lobe. Visual pathway: retina → optic nerve → optic chiasm (nasal fibers cross) → optic tract → lateral geniculate nucleus (thalamus) → optic radiation → primary visual cortex. Lesions at different points produce specific visual field defects. Macular sparing occurs with PCA occlusion (dual blood supply from PCA and MCA). Reference: Guyton — Visual Pathway.

questions.questionText: The pupillary light reflex pathway involves which cranial nerves?
questions.optionB: CN II (afferent) and CN III (efferent — parasympathetic)
questions.explanation: Pupillary light reflex: light → retina → CN II (optic nerve, afferent) → pretectal nucleus (both sides) → Edinger-Westphal nucleus → CN III parasympathetic fibers (efferent) → ciliary ganglion → sphincter pupillae → miosis. Both direct (same eye) and consensual (opposite eye) pupillary constriction occurs. RAPD (relative afferent pupillary defect/Marcus Gunn pupil): diminished direct response, intact consensual → optic nerve lesion. Reference: Guyton — Pupillary Reflex.
questions.conceptTag: Pupillary light reflex CN II III

questions.questionText: The limbic system is primarily involved in:
questions.optionB: Emotion, memory, motivation, and autonomic responses
questions.explanation: Limbic system structures: hippocampus (memory consolidation — declarative/explicit memory), amygdala (fear, emotion, emotional memory), hypothalamus (autonomic, endocrine), cingulate gyrus, septal nuclei, olfactory cortex. Bilateral hippocampal damage → anterograde amnesia (can't form new memories, e.g., patient HM). Amygdala damage → Klüver-Bucy syndrome (docility, hypersexuality, hyperorality, visual agnosia). Reference: Guyton — Limbic System.

questions.questionText: Weber test lateralizes to the affected ear in:
questions.explanation: Weber test (tuning fork on vertex): lateralizes TO the affected ear in conductive loss (external/middle ear block → bone conduction enhanced without air noise), AWAY from affected ear in sensorineural loss (inner ear/CN VIII damage). Rinne test: normally air conduction > bone conduction (positive). Conductive loss: BC > AC on affected side (negative Rinne). Sensorineural: AC > BC bilaterally but diminished on affected side. Reference: Guyton — Hearing Tests.
